part: Two Women, Two Mississippi Beginnings
title: Free State of Jones
summary: Emilye Crosby opens the December 1, 2015 joint interview at the University of Southern Mississippi with Jeanette Smith and Raylawni Branch, and Smith describes being born in 1940 in Jones County, the Free State of Jones, where Captain Newton Knight bought her great-great-grandmother Rachel as a slave, fell in love with her, deserted the Confederacy for the Union, and started the family documented in Victoria Bynum's book and a coming movie.

part: Two Women, Two Mississippi Beginnings
title: A Shotgun House on Whitney Street
summary: Branch tells of being born in 1941 in her grandmother's three-room shotgun house in Hattiesburg, with family roots in Mount Carmel near Prentiss where great-grandparents bought their own 80 acres, disputes the forty-acres-and-a-mule story as something that never really happened, and insists civil rights is still a present problem.

part: Never Taught to Hate
title: Poor White Folks Some People Call Them
summary: Branch describes growing up among blonde, blue-eyed kin so that she knew no racial difference until the death of Emmett Till, and recalls the poor white neighbor who helped her great-grandmother can and shell while her great-grandmother fed the woman's children, and the reciprocal relationships between women of color and the big houses in town.

part: Never Taught to Hate
title: Furniture in the Alley
topic: A Family's Hard Years in Chicago
summary: Branch recounts her fair-skinned mother being hired at Brock Candy Company because they thought she was white and fired on the spot for sitting to eat with the one Black woman there, her farmer father who died in Chicago, riding the L past the homes of Oak Park, and how, after he fell ill and her mother was pregnant, the family lost their home and ended up homeless with their furniture in the alley.

part: The Color Line in the Blood
title: Descendants of Newton Knight
summary: Smith explains that as descendants of Newton Knight her family had their own school and were called the lightning girls, that her father insisted his children attend public school and live in the real world without hatred for dark or white people, and that as a girl she peppered her mother with questions about being mixed and unaccepted on either side.

part: The Color Line in the Blood
title: I Want to Be Just an American
summary: Smith recalls being called a devil's child for her green eyes, having her hair pulled at school, wanting to be simply an American rather than black or white, her mother telling her not to dwell on the past, and how her grandmother, called butter gal for her blondeness, had been born after a plantation owner raped Smith's great-grandmother near Prentiss.

part: The Color Line in the Blood
title: Shotgun Houses From Camp Shelby
summary: Smith describes her great-grandmother buying one of the World War I shotgun houses moved up from Camp Shelby and lined beside the railroad tracks, and marrying a Cajun out of southern Louisiana, Frenchmen mixed with Native Americans and Africans, whom her grandmother stopped having children with after he was killed working the railroad in 1932.

part: The Color Line in the Blood
title: She Never Knew Her Real Name
summary: Smith tells how her mother kept her origins a taboo subject and died at sixty-seven never knowing her real name, listed on her birth certificate as Christine Holloway when the family name was Phanimae and her known name was after her Louisiana father, and reflects on the one-drop rule, being mistaken for Asian or Pacific Islander, and learning to love herself.

part: The Color Line in the Blood
title: The Store That Could Sell Guns
summary: Smith describes the timber land her grandmother inherited and gave on to children and grandchildren, the seasonal community of canning, syrup-making, and smoking meat, and her green-eyed, blonde uncle who, because of who his family was, was allowed to run a village store and sell guns and ammunition when Black people legally could not.

part: One of the First at Southern
title: The Armstrong-Branch Plaza
summary: Branch points to the Armstrong-Branch Plaza on the Southern campus, two blocks long with the international building she requested placed on it, the lecture series begun in the early 1990s, and says she would rather the money go to scholarships than things, before the talk turns to the recent February 10, 2013 tornado.

part: One of the First at Southern
title: We Didn't Have Any Problems at All
summary: Branch credits Vernon Dahmer, James Bourne and others for the path she and Gwendolyn Elaine Armstrong walked when they integrated the University of Southern Mississippi in 1965, contrasts the relative calm they met with the violence and deaths surrounding James Meredith at Ole Miss, and notes she was called a slur only once.

part: One of the First at Southern
title: Watching for My Tail
summary: Branch recalls white girls in the gym checking the mirror to see if she had a tail, being alphabetized to the back of the class and printed all the same color in the yearbook, her science-department advisor Dr. Fish, and President McCain being warned that Governor Ross Barnett would close the school if anyone was admitted, noting the McCain library they sit in and the plaza built out of guilt.

part: What Happened to Clyde Kennard
title: You Could Just Walk Up and Shoot Them
summary: Branch says that at the time a person could be shot with no real trial, citing the Eyes on the Prize footage of a white man shooting a Black would-be voter in McComb and the state representative Herbert Lee, and Smith says she knew Clyde Kennard personally from the restaurant on Mobile Street where he delivered eggs and leaders met at Smith's drugstore.

part: What Happened to Clyde Kennard
title: To Me They Were Sell-Outs
summary: Smith describes behind-the-scenes conversations in which Black principals Mr. Berger and Mr. Roy, wanting a Black junior college built in Hattiesburg, tried to talk Kennard out of coming, calling them sell-outs because the state-funded school should have been open to all, while McCain, a John Birch Society member, let Ole Miss and Meredith go first and called the women angels.

part: What Happened to Clyde Kennard
title: I Met Him in the Forrest County Jail
summary: Smith explains she knew Kennard a different way, meeting him sick in the Forrest County jail where her husband, the doctor the Moon brothers and NAACP wanted to examine him, found he needed care before he was sent to Parchman and checked out too late with advanced cancer; she recalls first meeting Raylawni Branch as a young NAACP secretary, defends her as a lifelong fighter, and places Kennard's home in the Macedonia, Kelly settlement community.

part: What Happened to Clyde Kennard
title: The Chicken-Feed Frame
summary: Smith recounts that Kennard came to Hattiesburg to help his widowed mother run a chicken farm and applied to the school three times amid talks involving McCain and the governor, that police arrested him first on a whiskey charge and then for stealing chicken feed, when in truth a young friend, Johnny Lee Roberts, swept up damp re-bagged feed and brought it because the feed houses refused to sell to Kennard.

part: What Happened to Clyde Kennard
title: A Comedy of a Court Case
topic: Seven Years for Trying to Enroll
summary: Smith tells how she and J.C. Fairley reached Dick Gregory and Charles Evers, brother of the slain Medgar Evers, who flew them on a Dick Gregory-funded plane to persuade the frightened nineteen-year-old witness to tell the truth, but the boy said whatever the lawyer said and Kennard got seven years, a man she says went to jail not for stealing but for trying to come to the school.

part: If It's Not Local, It's Not Real
title: The Backbone of Everything
summary: Smith credits the NAACP, with a branch in place since 1946 and men who filed a voting suit in the 1950s, as the backbone of integrating public accommodations and voting, laments that outsiders who came down briefly got the recognition while local people who risked their lives went uncredited, and repeats a Pennsylvania judge's line that if it's not local it's not real, recalling a journalist friend who fought in Hattiesburg yet lived in her own segregated community.

part: If It's Not Local, It's Not Real
title: The Only Race That Came Against Its Wishes
topic: Equal Rights and a Model President
summary: Smith, who counts herself one-quarter white, black, and Spanish, declines to profit from a casino-tribe enrollment because she wants only equal treatment, reflects that hers is the only race brought to America against its will, and holds up A. Philip Randolph and a president who answers cruelty without bitterness as models of restraint over self-defense.

part: If It's Not Local, It's Not Real
title: He Couldn't Swallow the Steak
summary: Circling back, Smith describes flying the terrified state witness to Chicago, where the group took him to dinner but he could not eat the steak with a white woman seated beside him and slipped away back to Hattiesburg, and she notes the Clyde Kennard Day now observed on campus and at the church where he is buried.

part: Born With a Mission
title: I Got the Principal Fired at Four and a Half
summary: Branch tells how at four and a half in Mount Carmel she walked off the stage and out of school after a principal whipped her for something she had not done, getting him fired that night, and recounts her great-grandmother, mother of two Pullman porters, riding free on a family pass to Federated Women's Club conventions and rebuking a conductor who called her Amy.

part: Born With a Mission
title: A Shotgun at the Door
summary: Branch describes learning to shoot as a matter of country survival and the shotgun kept at the door against intruders, even as she calls herself against guns and the NRA shameful, frames forgiveness as understanding people gripped by fear, and notes the gun her niece keeps in the woods for self-defense, before recalling that she left in 1966, the year Vernon Dahmer died.

part: A Stealth Campaign for Jobs
title: The First One Hired
summary: Branch recounts a stealth campaign before 1964: integrating a downtown hotel for a speaker, being the first person hired at the telephone company but unable to take nights with no childcare and a mentally ill husband gone, so Joyce Brown stepped up instead, and being the first clerk hired at Sears, hidden in shipping until she opened the candy counter for others.

part: A Stealth Campaign for Jobs
title: Climbing Across the Man's Desk
summary: Branch tells of becoming the first Black woman hired at Big Yank clothing, a plant of four hundred women who refused to work with her, after she climbed across the unemployment officer's desk demanding a job other than Miss Anne's kitchen, recruited three other women to run a permanent-press shift in competition with a nearby plant, stood on a box because she was told she was too short for the number-one machine, and walked home through the shadows to avoid being caught.

part: Emmett Till and the Things That Were Done
title: The Boy in the Casket
summary: Branch recounts a white child crawling under a Chicago church table to peer at her, the women saying nothing, and picking up the Jet Magazine with Emmett Till in his casket, an image that frightened her so much she ran all the way home, hardening her family's resolve, rooted in the protective shotgun of her country childhood, that no one would take one of their boys.

part: Emmett Till and the Things That Were Done
title: A Dark, Dingy Basement
summary: Branch and Smith describe Branch's husband building a clinic because as a Black doctor he had to hand hospital patients to a white doctor, the charity hospital in Laurel where Black patients were treated in a windowless basement past the furnace room, the privileged five o'clock appointments Smith's family got because of who her mother was, and Dr. Thacker's brick wall after the Klan threatened to kidnap his daughters for treating Black patients.

part: Fighting Long Before the Freedom Riders
title: Could Not Read or Write
summary: Branch explains a mother's fear most for the family's males, the bathrooms and hotels closed downtown, and her carpenter father who, literate with beautiful handwriting, was made a Navy instructor because most white Mississippians could not read or write, then turns to present-day recruiting problems, certificates-of-attendance GEDs, and jail become a business.

part: Fighting Long Before the Freedom Riders
title: They Never Gave Them Groceries
summary: Branch and Smith describe a fighting Forrest County NAACP of life members that drew outsiders, Smith's frustration that the visitors who wrote the books got the credit and funds while locals who housed and fed them were never reimbursed, and how from 1961 churches supplied food the women cooked on Mobile Street for hungry workers, tracing lifelong NAACP membership through grandmothers whose receipts were found tucked in their Bibles.

part: Fighting Long Before the Freedom Riders
title: He Pulled On Their Sheets All Day
summary: Smith, listed as crazy and unafraid of the police, explains that the Klan went to tear up other communities but feared Jones County because people there knew who was behind the sheets, recounts her grandfather donning a sheet to run off Klansmen headed to cut down Forrest County, and says she survived because her kin claimed her, with Newton Knight named as father on the birth certificate.

part: I Had Three Big Men at My Back
title: Lee Val Graves Went to Register
summary: Smith describes a flyer showing her grandfather Lee Val Graves at Theron Lynd's counter to register, recounts being among the first and youngest to register at twenty-one in 1961 with J.C. Fairley, James Bourne, and Vernon Dahmer at her back, and credits world-history teacher Marjorie James, later pulled to the white school for being a good teacher, with preparing the students so well that the towering, small-headed Lynd passed her constitutional answer without reading it.

part: I Had Three Big Men at My Back
title: He Was Going to Integrate Everything
summary: Branch tells how her husband, who came from Memphis through Meharry to pay off a state loan and stayed for the medical need, took his licensing tests on a freight elevator yet alongside everyone, filed a complaint with the federal government, and set out to integrate the hospitals and doctors' offices as soon as they took Medicare money, before she pivots to the head-start story.

part: A Busload of Children to Washington
title: Send Them to the White House
summary: Branch recounts telling Johnny Lee Roberts on the phone, with no car, no bus fare, and no daycare, that she ought to send her children to the White House for Lady Bird Johnson to mind, and how he found the money to fill a Greyhound with five hundred preschoolers sent to Washington in January, met there by women with proper winter clothing, after which Head Start came.

part: A Busload of Children to Washington
title: Not All White People Were Bad
summary: Branch says the one thing she learned from the students and outsiders who came was that not all white people were devils, crediting some who went back and did wonderful things, among them Jill Wakeman, who had stayed with a local woman, mailed Branch ten dollars a month through her two and a half years of nursing school in New York, and opened one of the biggest soup kitchens in San Francisco.

part: A Busload of Children to Washington
title: Thank My Daddy for Finding Me a Husband
topic: A Doctor at the Church Dedication
summary: Branch describes her husband working through an organization of Black doctors and a Black hospital where he transferred patients, how he met her when she came to a church dedication in Laurel after her father took pneumonia while she was bound for Delaware State, and how her father talked her up to the doctor until she married him within four months instead of college, reflecting that education is a continuing thing and a person remains a sponge for learning.
